2: Agricultural production in the developed world depends heavily on high inputs of fuel, water, and agro-chemicals yielding products of high carbon footprint (Clune et al., 2017). Mitigation of the im- pacts of agriculture on climate change, a prominent international

goal, requires drastic reductions of GHG emissions to limit the in- crease in temperature well below 2 °C at the end of the century, as outlined in the recent Paris Agreement (http://unfccc.int/paris_ agreement/items/9485.php) The ambitious EU "Roadmap 2050" aims at reducing emissions up to 80-95% by 2050 (http://www. roadmap2050.eu/project/roadmap-2050). Succeeding in the new policy environment requires the streamlining of methods and techniques for the accurate quantification and reduction of GHG emissions from agricultural systems (Haas et al., 2001; Smith et al., 2008; Litskas et al., 2011; Adewale et al., 2016; Zaher et al., 2016).

Abbreviations: NH3, Ammonia; CO2-equiv., CO2-equivalents; GHG, Greenhouse gas; ha, Hectares; Hp, Horsepower; IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change; ISO, International Organization for Standardization; LCA, Life Cycle Assessment; MT, Million tonnes; N20, Nitrous oxide; NO, Nitric oxide; PCF, Product carbon footprint.

Agricultural emissions in the EU reach 470.6 MT of CO2- equiv. per year, representing one tenth of the total emissions (Eurostat, 2016). The Union leads the world in wine production at 21.3 billion bottles per year, with vineyards covering more than 3 million ha (OIV, 2015). Production of a bottle of wine emits an average of 1.85 kg CO2-equiv. (e.g. Bosco et al., 2011; Vasquez-Rowe

et al., 2013; Pattara et al., 2017), and annual emissions from the sector reach close to 40 MT CO2-equiv. Viticulture contributes about 30% to the PCF for wine (Bosco et al., 2011; Point et al., 2012; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013) or close to 12 MT of CO2-equiv. per year.

The first step to reducing GHG emissions from agricultural production is identifying and reporting the PCF; the quantity of emissions per functional unit of the product (e.g. one kg of grapes or a bottle of wine) expressed as kg of CO2-equiv. The preferred methodology for PCF quantification is LCA (Bellon-Maurel et al., 2014; Adewale et al., 2016), whereby emissions from the full life cycle of the product, from material and energy inputs to final disposition are estimated and used to determine the PCF (e.g. Pattara et al., 2012; FIVS, 2016).

Wine PCF has been reported by several studies in recent years (e.g. Colman and Paster, 2009; Litskas et al., 2013; Navarro et al., 2017; Pattara et al., 2017) and the International Federation of Wines and Spirts has developed a publicly available International Wine Carbon Calculator (FIVS, 2016). However, soil carbon dy- namics have not been considered in detail for determining wine PCF. A notable advance has been the development of the Cool Farm Tool, (www.coolfarmtool.org), that takes into account the dynamics and management of carbon stocks in soils (Hillier et al., 2011). The tool offers the potential to the farmer to implement management scenarios and explore ways to reduce the PCF. In a comparison of 11 tools for PCF calculation, the Cool Farm Tool was identified as the best available in the public domain (Whittaker et al., 2013), but has never been used for determining the PCF of grapes.

Viticultural GHG emissions result mainly from cultivation practices (e.g. tillage, irrigation, fertilization, pest management) and soil processes (Point et al., 2012; Litskas et al., 2013; Vasquez- Rowe et al., 2013). Potential mitigation measures consist of: a) Increasing carbon sequestration in the soil and b) Reducing emis- sions from inputs such as field-energy use and agro-chemicals, through adoption of sustainable practices and/or the use of low input varieties.

Synthetic fertilizers represent the most carbon-intensive chemicals used in agriculture today, as they require vast amounts of energy for their industrial production and global transportation. Replacing part of synthetic fertilizers with organic, such as locally produced manure or compost leads to substantial savings in GHG emissions (Hillier et al., 2011; Pattara et al., 2017). In addition to providing nutrients to plants, organic fertilizers increase carbon sequestration in the soil. IPCC (2014) estimated that an additional 1.2 billion tonnes of carbon could be stocked every year in agri- cultural soils and substantially reduce GHG losses to the atmo- sphere (IPCC, 2014; UNFCCC, 2015). Studies that quantified carbon sequestration in vineyards (Marras et al., 2015; Eldon and Gershenson, 2015; Brunori et al., 2016) confirm the potential of organic fertilization to mitigate GHG emissions. Reduction in tillage frequency represents another mitigation strategy, as frequent cultivation accelerates organic matter decomposition and subse- quent CO2 loss to the atmosphere (Zomeni and Bruggeman, 2013; Eldon and Gershenson, 2015; Brunori et al., 2016).

Another key strategy for reducing viticultural GHG emissions is the use of varieties adapted to local conditions because they require fewer inputs (e.g. Litskas et al., 2013; Pattara et al., 2017). Intensive production practices release greater amounts of GHG (Point et al., 2012; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013; Pattara et al., 2017) and increase the PCF. Mediterranean islands represent a historic region for viticulture and wine production, for more than 5,500 years now, as evidenced from wine residues identified in pots from the island of Cyprus (Lentini, 2009). Selection over millennia yielded grape va- rieties adapted to the climate of the islands, such as Nero d' Avola in Sicily, Mosxato Spinas in Crete, Atheri in Rhodes and Xynisteri in

Cyprus. Beginning in the 80' and 90's, however, the varietal make up of vineyards shifted towards more commercial varieties, such as Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot and Chardonnay that require more inputs and are more carbon intensive (Point et al., 2012; Vasquez- Rowe et al., 2013).

The aims of the current study were to identify carbon emissions and pin-point carbon hotspots for a local (Xynisteri) and an intro- duced wine variety (Cabernet Sauvignon) as well as a table grape variety (Thomson seedless). We used as a case study the Mediter- ranean island of Cyprus, with more than 5,500 years of wine his- tory. The inclusion of soil carbon dynamics in the PCF determination enabled us to investigate an economically feasible scenario for PCF reduction through decreasing fertilizer use and increasing soil carbon sequestration. The study provides new in- sights for GHG emissions reduction in viticulture and agricultural production in general.

Mediterranean islands represent a historic region for viticulture and wine production, for more than 5,500 years now, as evidenced from wine residues identified in pots from the island of Cyprus (Lentini, 2009). Selection over millennia yielded grape va- rieties adapted to the climate of the islands, such as Nero d' Avola in Sicily, Mosxato Spinas in Crete, Atheri in Rhodes and Xynisteri in

Cyprus. Beginning in the 80' and 90's, however, the varietal make up of vineyards shifted towards more commercial varieties, such as Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot and Chardonnay that require more inputs and are more carbon intensive (Point et al., 2012; Vasquez- Rowe et al., 2013).

The aims of the current study were to identify carbon emissions and pin-point carbon hotspots for a local (Xynisteri) and an intro- duced wine variety (Cabernet Sauvignon) as well as a table grape variety (Thomson seedless). We used as a case study the Mediter- ranean island of Cyprus, with more than 5,500 years of wine his- tory. The inclusion of soil carbon dynamics in the PCF determination enabled us to investigate an economically feasible scenario for PCF reduction through decreasing fertilizer use and increasing soil carbon sequestration. The study provides new in- sights for GHG emissions reduction in viticulture and agricultural production in general.
6: Agricultural production in the developed world depends heavily on high inputs of fuel, water, and agro-chemicals yielding products of high carbon footprint (Clune et al., 2017). Mitigation of the im- pacts of agriculture on climate change, a prominent international

goal, requires drastic reductions of GHG emissions to limit the in- crease in temperature well below 2 °C at the end of the century, as outlined in the recent Paris Agreement (http://unfccc.int/paris_ agreement/items/9485.php) The ambitious EU "Roadmap 2050" aims at reducing emissions up to 80-95% by 2050 (http://www. roadmap2050.eu/project/roadmap-2050). Succeeding in the new policy environment requires the streamlining of methods and techniques for the accurate quantification and reduction of GHG emissions from agricultural systems (Haas et al., 2001; Smith et al., 2008; Litskas et al., 2011; Adewale et al., 2016; Zaher et al., 2016).

Abbreviations: NH3, Ammonia; CO2-equiv., CO2-equivalents; GHG, Greenhouse gas; ha, Hectares; Hp, Horsepower; IPCC, Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change; ISO, International Organization for Standardization; LCA, Life Cycle Assessment; MT, Million tonnes; N20, Nitrous oxide; NO, Nitric oxide; PCF, Product carbon footprint.

Agricultural emissions in the EU reach 470.6 MT of CO2- equiv. per year, representing one tenth of the total emissions (Eurostat, 2016). The Union leads the world in wine production at 21.3 billion bottles per year, with vineyards covering more than 3 million ha (OIV, 2015). Production of a bottle of wine emits an average of 1.85 kg CO2-equiv. (e.g. Bosco et al., 2011; Vasquez-Rowe

et al., 2013; Pattara et al., 2017), and annual emissions from the sector reach close to 40 MT CO2-equiv. Viticulture contributes about 30% to the PCF for wine (Bosco et al., 2011; Point et al., 2012; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013) or close to 12 MT of CO2-equiv. per year.

The first step to reducing GHG emissions from agricultural production is identifying and reporting the PCF; the quantity of emissions per functional unit of the product (e.g. one kg of grapes or a bottle of wine) expressed as kg of CO2-equiv. The preferred methodology for PCF quantification is LCA (Bellon-Maurel et al., 2014; Adewale et al., 2016), whereby emissions from the full life cycle of the product, from material and energy inputs to final disposition are estimated and used to determine the PCF (e.g. Pattara et al., 2012; FIVS, 2016).

Wine PCF has been reported by several studies in recent years (e.g. Colman and Paster, 2009; Litskas et al., 2013; Navarro et al., 2017; Pattara et al., 2017) and the International Federation of Wines and Spirts has developed a publicly available International Wine Carbon Calculator (FIVS, 2016). However, soil carbon dy- namics have not been considered in detail for determining wine PCF. A notable advance has been the development of the Cool Farm Tool, (www.coolfarmtool.org), that takes into account the dynamics and management of carbon stocks in soils (Hillier et al., 2011). The tool offers the potential to the farmer to implement management scenarios and explore ways to reduce the PCF. In a comparison of 11 tools for PCF calculation, the Cool Farm Tool was identified as the best available in the public domain (Whittaker et al., 2013), but has never been used for determining the PCF of grapes.

Viticultural GHG emissions result mainly from cultivation practices (e.g. tillage, irrigation, fertilization, pest management) and soil processes (Point et al., 2012; Litskas et al., 2013; Vasquez- Rowe et al., 2013). Potential mitigation measures consist of: a) Increasing carbon sequestration in the soil and b) Reducing emis- sions from inputs such as field-energy use and agro-chemicals, through adoption of sustainable practices and/or the use of low input varieties.

Synthetic fertilizers represent the most carbon-intensive chemicals used in agriculture today, as they require vast amounts of energy for their industrial production and global transportation. Replacing part of synthetic fertilizers with organic, such as locally produced manure or compost leads to substantial savings in GHG emissions (Hillier et al., 2011; Pattara et al., 2017). In addition to providing nutrients to plants, organic fertilizers increase carbon sequestration in the soil. IPCC (2014) estimated that an additional 1.2 billion tonnes of carbon could be stocked every year in agri- cultural soils and substantially reduce GHG losses to the atmo- sphere (IPCC, 2014; UNFCCC, 2015). Studies that quantified carbon sequestration in vineyards (Marras et al., 2015; Eldon and Gershenson, 2015; Brunori et al., 2016) confirm the potential of organic fertilization to mitigate GHG emissions. Reduction in tillage frequency represents another mitigation strategy, as frequent cultivation accelerates organic matter decomposition and subse- quent CO2 loss to the atmosphere (Zomeni and Bruggeman, 2013; Eldon and Gershenson, 2015; Brunori et al., 2016).

Another key strategy for reducing viticultural GHG emissions is the use of varieties adapted to local conditions because they require fewer inputs (e.g. Litskas et al., 2013; Pattara et al., 2017). Intensive production practices release greater amounts of GHG (Point et al., 2012; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013; Pattara et al., 2017) and increase the PCF. Mediterranean islands represent a historic region for viticulture and wine production, for more than 5,500 years now, as evidenced from wine residues identified in pots from the island of Cyprus (Lentini, 2009). Selection over millennia yielded grape va- rieties adapted to the climate of the islands, such as Nero d' Avola in Sicily, Mosxato Spinas in Crete, Atheri in Rhodes and Xynisteri in

Cyprus. Beginning in the 80' and 90's, however, the varietal make up of vineyards shifted towards more commercial varieties, such as Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot and Chardonnay that require more inputs and are more carbon intensive (Point et al., 2012; Vasquez- Rowe et al., 2013).

The aims of the current study were to identify carbon emissions and pin-point carbon hotspots for a local (Xynisteri) and an intro- duced wine variety (Cabernet Sauvignon) as well as a table grape variety (Thomson seedless). We used as a case study the Mediter- ranean island of Cyprus, with more than 5,500 years of wine his- tory. The inclusion of soil carbon dynamics in the PCF determination enabled us to investigate an economically feasible scenario for PCF reduction through decreasing fertilizer use and increasing soil carbon sequestration. The study provides new in- sights for GHG emissions reduction in viticulture and agricultural production in general.

10: 2.1. Grape varieties and data collection for the research

Vineyards cover 7,000 ha on the island of Cyprus, with more than 60% of the area planted to two indigenous varieties Mavro (red) and Xynisteri (white). The study included the local white variety Xynisteri, and two introduced varieties, the red Cabernet Sauvignon, and the white table grape variety Soultanina (also called Soultana or Thomson seedless). Xynisteri uses include the pro- duction of white wine and most importantly the mixing with the local red variety Mavro for the production of the sweet desert wine Commandaria (Protected Designation of Origin), whose name and production method remains essentially the same since 800 BCE. Cabernet Sauvignon is of French origin, and it is the most popular red winemaking variety in the world, covering 290,000 ha, or 6.3% of the global area devoted to vineyards (Anderson and Aryal, 2014). Soultanina is mainly used for production of table grapes. The three varieties represent close to one third of the ca. 7,000 ha of vineyards of the island, with 363, 287 and 1,937 ha for each of Soultanina, C. Sauvignon and Xynisteri, respectively (Cystat, 2016).

Ninety (90) farms, 30 for each variety were selected in the Districts of Limassol and Paphos (Fig.1 where more than 80% of the vineyards of the island are located. The vineyards were selected to capture differences in management practices, microclimatic con- ditions, soil types, materials and equipment used. A questionnaire was designed based on the LCA approach (ISO, 1997; ISO, 1998; ISO, 2006) to interview the farmers and gather the data required for the PCF calculations. Agricultural professionals (agronomists, wine- makers) were also consulted to validate the data collected from farmers. Table 1 presents the main management practices and in- puts in vineyards based on questionnaire results.

12: <header>2.2. Carbon footprint calculations for cypriot viticulture </header>

The online Cool Farm Tool (www.coolfarmtool.org) was used to calculate GHG emissions and carbon balance in vineyard soils using data collected from the questionnaires. The tool follows the prin- ciples of LCA and the framework provided in IPCC (2006) for GHG monitoring and reporting. The following factors were considered for PCF (kg CO2-equiv./kg of grapes) calculation: 1) Fertilizers: GHG emissions from the production and distribution of fertilizers, 2) N20, NO and NH3 soil emissions as a result of nitrogen fertilizer application and N transformation processes in soils, 3) Pesticide production, 4) Crop residue management, 5) Carbon stock changes in the vineyard soil after organic matter amendments and degra- dation, 6) Field energy use for irrigation, pruning, tillage, pesticide

Management practice|Variety|| Management practice|Xynisteri|Cabernet Sauvignon|Soultanina Crop Residues from Pruning Fertilizers|Pruning once per year. 150-1,500 kg/ha residues. 250 kg/ha: N-P-K 15-15-15 application by hand. Less than 10% of farmers apply irrigation. 3 times a year ploughing using a 4 Hp hand driven or a four wheel (20-65 Hp) tractor to control weeds. Light truck or tractor.|Sulphur 1-3 times/year and 4-6 times/ year folpet and mancozeb fungicides for powdery and downy mildew and spinosad insecticide 1-6 times/year against the European grapevine moth. 3-10 times/year May-September (70 -140 mm/event) using the public irrigation network (electricity for the pumps). More than 75% of farmers irrigate. 3-5 times a year ploughing using a 4 Hp hand driven or a four wheel (20-65 Hp) tractor to control weeds. and fertilizer application and manure transportation, and 7) Off- farm transport (transportation to the winery/market and annual trips of farmers to the vineyards). The system boundary for the study was from vine to the winery/market door. Emission factors used to calculate the PCF are presented in Table S1 (Supplementary material). For fertilizers, emissions from the production and distribution, fossil fuel consumption for manufacturing and transport from the factory to the vineyards were taken into account. For the estima- tions of N20, NO and NH3 emissions related to nitrogen fertilizer and manure application, the model of Bouwman et al. (2002) was

used as presented in Hillier et al. (2011). The model takes into ac- count physicochemical soil properties (e.g. texture, pH, water holding capacity, drainage) and climate parameters to determine emissions from soils. The N20, NO and NH3 soil emissions (in kg/ha) related to the application of nitrogen fertilizers/manure in vine- yards are estimated from the following empirical equations: (1) In the equation C is a constant and F(i) stands for Factor classes which are: fertilizer type X fertilizer application rate, crop type, soil

texture, soil organic carbon, soil drainage, soil pH, soil cation ex- change capacity, climate type and application method (Table S1). NO emissions are estimated using Equation (1) but with different parameter values (Table S1).
Ammonia emissions are estimated as follows:

FA represents the amount of fertilizer applied (kg/ha). F(i) stands for Factor classes which are the same as for Equation (1). Parame- ters for Equation (2) are also presented in Table S1. NO and NH3 emissions were converted to N20 by the factor 0.01 as outlined in IPCC (2006).

For emissions related to residue management (i.e. kg of pruning produced) the factors presented in Hillier et al. (2011) were used, accounting for different management options, such as burning, leaving untreated on the vineyard surface or incorporating into the soil.

For carbon stock changes in the vineyard soil and the carbon balance in the field the IPCC tier 1 approach was used as described in Ogle et al. (2005) and Hillier et al. (2011). After quantification, the change in carbon stocks was converted to annual CO2 emissions. The influence of the addition of organic amendments (i.e. manure) to soil organic carbon was estimated in accordance to Smith et al. (1997) using the empirical equation:
where %SOC is the percentage of change in the soil organic carbon and FYM the dry weight (in tonnes/ha) of manure added.

Data for the accurate PCF determination of pesticide applica- tions are scarce, and we followed the approach of Hillier et al. (2011), Litskas et al. (2013) and Vasquez-Rowe et al. (2013), whereby a standard value of 20.5 kg CO2 equiv./ha is used per product application. For emissions related to field energy use the model presented in the American Society of Agricultural and Bio- logical Engineers technical standards (ASABE, 2006a, 2006b) for fuel use as a function of machinery operation was applied. Country specific grid electricity emissions were taken from the GHG pro- tocol (http://www.ghgprotocol.org/) The emissions related to transportation were calculated after obtaining the fuel consump- tion and using the emission factors from Hillier et al. (2011) and FIVS (2016) for the vehicles that were used in each of the farms. The factors typically include both emissions from fuel burning and emissions from extraction and mining or processing.
13: <header>2.2. Carbon footprint calculations for cypriot viticulture </header>

The online Cool Farm Tool (www.coolfarmtool.org) was used to calculate GHG emissions and carbon balance in vineyard soils using data collected from the questionnaires. The tool follows the prin- ciples of LCA and the framework provided in IPCC (2006) for GHG monitoring and reporting. The following factors were considered for PCF (kg CO2-equiv./kg of grapes) calculation: 1) Fertilizers: GHG emissions from the production and distribution of fertilizers, 2) N20, NO and NH3 soil emissions as a result of nitrogen fertilizer application and N transformation processes in soils, 3) Pesticide production, 4) Crop residue management, 5) Carbon stock changes in the vineyard soil after organic matter amendments and degra- dation, 6) Field energy use for irrigation, pruning, tillage, pesticide

and fertilizer application and manure transportation, and 7) Off- farm transport (transportation to the winery/market and annual trips of farmers to the vineyards). The system boundary for the study was from vine to the winery/market door. Emission factors used to calculate the PCF are presented in Table S1 (Supplementary material). For fertilizers, emissions from the production and distribution, fossil fuel consumption for manufacturing and transport from the factory to the vineyards were taken into account. For the estima- tions of N20, NO and NH3 emissions related to nitrogen fertilizer and manure application, the model of Bouwman et al. (2002) was

used as presented in Hillier et al. (2011). The model takes into ac- count physicochemical soil properties (e.g. texture, pH, water holding capacity, drainage) and climate parameters to determine emissions from soils. The N20, NO and NH3 soil emissions (in kg/ha) related to the application of nitrogen fertilizers/manure in vine- yards are estimated from the following empirical equations: (1) In the equation C is a constant and F(i) stands for Factor classes which are: fertilizer type X fertilizer application rate, crop type, soil

FA represents the amount of fertilizer applied (kg/ha). F(i) stands for Factor classes which are the same as for Equation (1). Parame- ters for Equation (2) are also presented in Table S1. NO and NH3 emissions were converted to N20 by the factor 0.01 as outlined in IPCC (2006).

For emissions related to residue management (i.e. kg of pruning produced) the factors presented in Hillier et al. (2011) were used, accounting for different management options, such as burning, leaving untreated on the vineyard surface or incorporating into the soil.

For carbon stock changes in the vineyard soil and the carbon balance in the field the IPCC tier 1 approach was used as described in Ogle et al. (2005) and Hillier et al. (2011). After quantification, the change in carbon stocks was converted to annual CO2 emissions. where %SOC is the percentage of change in the soil organic carbon and FYM the dry weight (in tonnes/ha) of manure added.

Data for the accurate PCF determination of pesticide applica- tions are scarce, and we followed the approach of Hillier et al. (2011), Litskas et al. (2013) and Vasquez-Rowe et al. (2013), whereby a standard value of 20.5 kg CO2 equiv./ha is used per product application. For emissions related to field energy use the model presented in the American Society of Agricultural and Bio- logical Engineers technical standards (ASABE, 2006a, 2006b) for fuel use as a function of machinery operation was applied. Country specific grid electricity emissions were taken from the GHG pro- tocol (http://www.ghgprotocol.org/) The emissions related to transportation were calculated after obtaining the fuel consump- tion and using the emission factors from Hillier et al. (2011) and FIVS (2016) for the vehicles that were used in each of the farms. The factors typically include both emissions from fuel burning and emissions from extraction and mining or processing.
17: <header>2.2. Carbon footprint calculations for cypriot viticulture </header>

The online Cool Farm Tool (www.coolfarmtool.org) was used to calculate GHG emissions and carbon balance in vineyard soils using data collected from the questionnaires. The tool follows the prin- ciples of LCA and the framework provided in IPCC (2006) for GHG monitoring and reporting. The following factors were considered for PCF (kg CO2-equiv./kg of grapes) calculation: 1) Fertilizers: GHG emissions from the production and distribution of fertilizers, 2) N20, NO and NH3 soil emissions as a result of nitrogen fertilizer application and N transformation processes in soils, 3) Pesticide production, 4) Crop residue management, 5) Carbon stock changes in the vineyard soil after organic matter amendments and degra- dation, 6) Field energy use for irrigation, pruning, tillage, pesticide

19: Summary statistics (average, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, standardized skewness and kurtosis) were calculated for the data obtained for each of the components of PCF described in section 2.2 using the STATGRAPHICS Centurion XVI (v. 16.1.11) software. The Levene's test was performed to test for departures from the equality of variances between groups. Since no major departures from the assumption of normality and variance-equality were detected, we used one-way ANOVA to compare treatment means. The Tukey's honestly significant difference (HSD) procedure was used for multiple comparisons at the 95% confidence level. The visualization of the results was done by constructing Tukey style Box-and-Whisker Plots as proposed in Krzywinski and Altman (2014).
20: Carbon storage into the vineyard soil through incorporation of

organic amendments is a feasible option for reducing grape PCF. Furthermore, organic amendment use has the added benefit of reducing emissions from fertilizer use and soil N20 that represent 45-66% of the grape PCF of the three varieties (Figs. 2 and 3). A 50% reduction for the use of synthetic fertilizers was considered as feasible after inquiring the farmers for their willingness to shift towards organic fertilizers, as they were sceptical about a full shift to organic fertilization. Synthetic fertilizers can be replaced by sheep and goat manure (0.7% N, 0.14% P, 0.7% K) in amounts high enough to provide the required nutrients to the vines. Manure has a very low cost or is freely available in the area and its uncontrolled disposal to natural land results to contamination through mineral leaching. Consequently, the farmers were asked if they have the possibility to obtain animal manure, in what amounts, which is the distance between the vineyard and the place where the manure is produced and if they have the required equipment to transport and incorporate it. GHG emissions due to the transportation and incorporation of the manure into the vineyard soil were taken into account based on the machinery used for transportation and dis- tance from the vineyard to calculate fuel consumption.

In addition, farmers were inquired about the possibility to reduce by one application the frequency of soil tillage which is practiced mainly for weed control. GHG emissions mitigation due to decreased soil tillage frequency was quantified. The scenario for PCF reduction for each farm was specifically tailored to be as realistic as possible, and the PCF was recalculated following the methodology presented in section 2.2.

23: The average PCF (+ 1 standard deviation) for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina was 0.283 +0.350), 0.572 (+ 0.580) and 0.846 ( 0.229) kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, respectively. Significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 14.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2a-g). Soultanina had the highest PCF because of the increased use of fertilizers, pesticides and field en- ergy (e.g. electricity for irrigation and fuel for transportation) (Fig. 2b, d, f and Fig. 3). Producers of table grapes use high amounts of fertilizers and irrigation water to achieve the highest possible yield which usually corresponds to increased profit. The local Xynisteri on the other hand, a variety adapted to the dry climatic conditions and less fertile soils of Cyprus, had the lowest PCF (Fig. 2a). The PCF for the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon was in- termediate, as the variety requires more inputs than Xynisteri to achieve the desired yield quantity and quality characteristics. The average yield for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina based on questionnaire answers was 4,240 (+ 1,230), 6,800 (+ 890) and 11,500 (+2,230) kg/ha, respectively.

The contribution of fertilizer production to the PCF of grapes was 0.113 (+ 0.194), 0.218 (+ 0.292) and 0.233 ( 0.081) kg CO2- equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 2b). Significant differences were observed among Xynisteri and the other two varieties (F = 5.78, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2b). Increased use of fertilizers in Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina corresponds to the more intensive management of the two varieties than Xynisteri. Field N20 emissions from the use of nitrogen fertilizers were estimated at 0.061 (+ 0.114), 0.159 ( 0.200) and 0.147 0.064) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions were signifi- cantly higher for Soultanina and Cabernet Sauvignon than for Xynisteri (F = 4.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2c). The increased values for Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina stem from the use of ammonium nitrate and urea fertilizers at higher annual doses than

Fig. 2. Box-and-Whisker plots for grape PCF. Different letters above the box-plots indicate statistically significant differences among the means (P < 0.05) after Tukey HSD following one-way ANOVA. a) PCF, b) fertilizer production, c) field N20 emissions, d) pesticides, e) crop residue management, f) field energy and g) transportation. Note the different scales for the y-axis.

for Xynisteri (Table 1). Emissions from fertilizer production and associated N20 release from the soil have the highest share for the PCF of grape production in Cyprus at 59, 66 and 45% for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 3).

Pesticide use contributed 0.015 (+ 0.018), 0.028 (+ 0.039) and 0.034 (+ 0.015) kg CO2-equiv./kg to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from pesticide use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 4.37, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2d). Increased use of pesticides for Soultanina reflects the extremely low damage thresholds for table grapes. In Cabernet Sauvignon, higher pesticide use than for Xynisteri, may reflect a higher sus- ceptibility of the former to diseases (Table 1).

Crop residue management added to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina an estimated 0.039 (+ 0.056), 0.050 (+ 0.064) and 0.048 (+ 0.043) kg CO2-equiv./kg, respectively. No significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 0.31, df = 2,87, P > 0.05, Fig. 2e), as farmers follow similar practices (Table 1). The worst case scenario for residue manage- ment is to left it decompose in piles, because of the fast release of methane and N20 gasses, which have a greater global warming potential than CO2. Burning of residues is a common practice in Cyprus.

Field energy use contributed to the product PCF 0.034 (+ 0.031), 0.088 (+ 0.055) and 0.343 (+ 0.168) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from field energy use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 74.84, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The increased use of energy for Soultanina is mainly

attributed to electrical energy used for irrigation. Table grapes are irrigated weekly from June to August to increase yields and improve product quality. Irrigation in Cabernet Sauvignon is less frequent than Soultanina vineyards, whereas the local Xynisteri receives irrigation rarely.

The contribution of transportation to the PCF was 0.031 (+ 0.128), 0.026 (+ 0.019) and 0.034 (+ 0.020) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Sig- nificant differences were observed between Xynisteri and Soulta- nina (F = 3.67, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The mean of transportation for Xynisteri was mainly driven by two vineyards (PCF contribution at 0.204 and 0.679 - not shown on Fig. 2f due to axis scale size limitations) with a large distance from wineries. Increased emissions from transportation for Soultanina stem from more frequent farmer field trips to the vineyards for inspections than for the other two varieties (Table 1) as well as a higher number of trips to deliver the product to the markets. In the case of Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon there is only one or two trips to carry the product to the winery and the farmers visit the vineyards fewer times during the growing season than for Soultanina (Table 1).

Only a few studies attempted to estimate the PCF of viticulture (e.g. Kavargiris et al., 2009; Bosco et al., 2011; Point et al., 2012; Litskas et al., 2013; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013; Villanueva-Rey et al., 2014; Pattara et al., 2017), but none focused specifically on the potential of applying management techniques to reduce the PCF. Kavargiris et al. (2009) and Litskas et al. (2013) focused more on energy balance for grape production with emissions from burning fossil fuels only considered for PCF quantification. Although very important, carbon stock balance, N transformations

Fig. 3. Contribution of management practices to the PCF of grape production for (a) the local variety Xynisteri, (b) the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon and (c) the introduced table grape variety Soultanina.

in the soil and residue management were not taken into account. Litskas et al. (2013), estimated the average PCF for Xynisteri grapes at 0.155 kg CO2-equiv./kg, which is lower than the one re- ported in the current study. In another study, Vasquez-Rowe et al. (2013) reported a PCF range for grapes from 0.113 to 1.613 kg CO2-equiv./kg for vineyards in Luxembourg, Italy and Spain, respectively. Diesel fuel (field energy use) and fertilizer use were the carbon hotspots. The increased PCF for some varieties, such as Ribeiro, Cannonau and Rias Baixas was attributed to increased fertilizer consumption. Bosco et al. (2011) reported a PCF range for grape production equal to 0.150-0.300 kg CO2-equiv./kg from the Tuscan area in Italy which was equal to 22% of the PCF of a bottle of wine. In the Bosco et al. (2011) study, the planting of vineyards and trellis infrastructure contributed significantly to the GHG emissions from viticulture. Additionally, the use of nitrogen fertilizers and

N20 emissions as well as grapes harvesting and transport were the parameters that mostly affected the carbon footprint in the agri- cultural phase. Point et al. (2012) in their research for the PCF of grapes cultivated in Nova Scotia, Canada, also reported that fertil- izers and fuels were the main contributors to emissions in Char- donnay and Pinot Noir varieties yielding a PCF of 0.8 kg CO2-equiv./ kg grapes.

Environmentally friendly systems could pave the way for lowering the PCF of grape production. In Spain, Villanueva-Rey et al. (2014) found that biodynamic production resulted in lower PCF and environmental burden than conventional agricultural practices in vineyards. The biodynamic site in the study had an 80% decrease in diesel inputs, due to a lower application of plant protection prod- ucts and fertilizers, and the introduction of manual work rather than mechanised activities. However, carbon stock changes, N transformations in soil and residue management were not taken into account.

Coupling the PCF of grapes with average yield per ha enables the estimation of total GHG emissions from vine cultivation in Cyprus at 2,638, 2,848 and 7,884 kg CO2-equiv./ha for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Whittaker et al. (2013) used the Cool Farm Tool to calculate emissions from arable crops, either for food or bioenergy production in the UK. Even though Mediterranean viticulture is generally considered a low GHG emissions activity, the PCF of Soultanina in the current study is 1.3 times higher than annual crops (e.g. wheat) in the UK and 1.4 times higher than fruits (plum, apricot, peach) cultivated in the Medi- terranean region (Clune et al., 2017).

Adopting a common procedure for accounting and reporting the PCF of agricultural products enables the comparison among the different products and management strategies. This will be the first step for designing effective policies and strategies to reduce the PCF for the agricultural sector. The accounting of carbon stock changes in agriculture is of major importance for GHG emissions mitigation at a global scale, something that was recently highlighted by the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC, 2015).
24: The average PCF (+ 1 standard deviation) for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina was 0.283 +0.350), 0.572 (+ 0.580) and 0.846 ( 0.229) kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, respectively. Significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 14.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2a-g). Soultanina had the highest PCF because of the increased use of fertilizers, pesticides and field en- ergy (e.g. electricity for irrigation and fuel for transportation) (Fig. 2b, d, f and Fig. 3). Producers of table grapes use high amounts of fertilizers and irrigation water to achieve the highest possible yield which usually corresponds to increased profit. The local Xynisteri on the other hand, a variety adapted to the dry climatic conditions and less fertile soils of Cyprus, had the lowest PCF (Fig. 2a). The PCF for the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon was in- termediate, as the variety requires more inputs than Xynisteri to achieve the desired yield quantity and quality characteristics. The average yield for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina based on questionnaire answers was 4,240 (+ 1,230), 6,800 (+ 890) and 11,500 (+2,230) kg/ha, respectively.

The contribution of fertilizer production to the PCF of grapes was 0.113 (+ 0.194), 0.218 (+ 0.292) and 0.233 ( 0.081) kg CO2- equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 2b). Significant differences were observed among Xynisteri and the other two varieties (F = 5.78, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2b). Increased use of fertilizers in Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina corresponds to the more intensive management of the two varieties than Xynisteri. Field N20 emissions from the use of nitrogen fertilizers were estimated at 0.061 (+ 0.114), 0.159 ( 0.200) and 0.147 0.064) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions were signifi- cantly higher for Soultanina and Cabernet Sauvignon than for Xynisteri (F = 4.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2c). The increased values for Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina stem from the use of ammonium nitrate and urea fertilizers at higher annual doses than

Fig. 2. Box-and-Whisker plots for grape PCF. Different letters above the box-plots indicate statistically significant differences among the means (P < 0.05) after Tukey HSD following one-way ANOVA. a) PCF, b) fertilizer production, c) field N20 emissions, d) pesticides, e) crop residue management, f) field energy and g) transportation. Note the different scales for the y-axis.

for Xynisteri (Table 1). Emissions from fertilizer production and associated N20 release from the soil have the highest share for the PCF of grape production in Cyprus at 59, 66 and 45% for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 3).

Pesticide use contributed 0.015 (+ 0.018), 0.028 (+ 0.039) and 0.034 (+ 0.015) kg CO2-equiv./kg to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from pesticide use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 4.37, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2d). Increased use of pesticides for Soultanina reflects the extremely low damage thresholds for table grapes. In Cabernet Sauvignon, higher pesticide use than for Xynisteri, may reflect a higher sus- ceptibility of the former to diseases (Table 1).

Crop residue management added to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina an estimated 0.039 (+ 0.056), 0.050 (+ 0.064) and 0.048 (+ 0.043) kg CO2-equiv./kg, respectively. No significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 0.31, df = 2,87, P > 0.05, Fig. 2e), as farmers follow similar practices (Table 1). The worst case scenario for residue manage- ment is to left it decompose in piles, because of the fast release of methane and N20 gasses, which have a greater global warming potential than CO2. Burning of residues is a common practice in Cyprus.

Field energy use contributed to the product PCF 0.034 (+ 0.031), 0.088 (+ 0.055) and 0.343 (+ 0.168) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from field energy use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 74.84, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The increased use of energy for Soultanina is mainly

attributed to electrical energy used for irrigation. Table grapes are irrigated weekly from June to August to increase yields and improve product quality. Irrigation in Cabernet Sauvignon is less frequent than Soultanina vineyards, whereas the local Xynisteri receives irrigation rarely.

The contribution of transportation to the PCF was 0.031 (+ 0.128), 0.026 (+ 0.019) and 0.034 (+ 0.020) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Sig- nificant differences were observed between Xynisteri and Soulta- nina (F = 3.67, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The mean of transportation for Xynisteri was mainly driven by two vineyards (PCF contribution at 0.204 and 0.679 - not shown on Fig. 2f due to axis scale size limitations) with a large distance from wineries. Increased emissions from transportation for Soultanina stem from more frequent farmer field trips to the vineyards for inspections than for the other two varieties (Table 1) as well as a higher number of trips to deliver the product to the markets. In the case of Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon there is only one or two trips to carry the product to the winery and the farmers visit the vineyards fewer times during the growing season than for Soultanina (Table 1).

Only a few studies attempted to estimate the PCF of viticulture (e.g. Kavargiris et al., 2009; Bosco et al., 2011; Point et al., 2012; Litskas et al., 2013; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013; Villanueva-Rey et al., 2014; Pattara et al., 2017), but none focused specifically on the potential of applying management techniques to reduce the PCF. Kavargiris et al. (2009) and Litskas et al. (2013) focused more on energy balance for grape production with emissions from burning fossil fuels only considered for PCF quantification. Although very important, carbon stock balance, N transformations

Environmentally friendly systems could pave the way for lowering the PCF of grape production. In Spain, Villanueva-Rey et al. (2014) found that biodynamic production resulted in lower PCF and environmental burden than conventional agricultural practices in vineyards. The biodynamic site in the study had an 80% decrease in diesel inputs, due to a lower application of plant protection prod- ucts and fertilizers, and the introduction of manual work rather than mechanised activities. However, carbon stock changes, N transformations in soil and residue management were not taken into account.

Coupling the PCF of grapes with average yield per ha enables the estimation of total GHG emissions from vine cultivation in Cyprus at 2,638, 2,848 and 7,884 kg CO2-equiv./ha for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Whittaker et al. (2013) used the Cool Farm Tool to calculate emissions from arable crops, either for food or bioenergy production in the UK. Even though Mediterranean viticulture is generally considered a low GHG emissions activity, the PCF of Soultanina in the current study is 1.3 times higher than annual crops (e.g. wheat) in the UK and 1.4 times higher than fruits (plum, apricot, peach) cultivated in the Medi- terranean region (Clune et al., 2017).

Adopting a common procedure for accounting and reporting the PCF of agricultural products enables the comparison among the different products and management strategies. This will be the first step for designing effective policies and strategies to reduce the PCF for the agricultural sector. The accounting of carbon stock changes in agriculture is of major importance for GHG emissions mitigation at a global scale, something that was recently highlighted by the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC, 2015).
27: The average PCF (+ 1 standard deviation) for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina was 0.283 +0.350), 0.572 (+ 0.580) and 0.846 ( 0.229) kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, respectively. Significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 14.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2a-g). Soultanina had the highest PCF because of the increased use of fertilizers, pesticides and field en- ergy (e.g. electricity for irrigation and fuel for transportation) (Fig. 2b, d, f and Fig. 3). Producers of table grapes use high amounts of fertilizers and irrigation water to achieve the highest possible yield which usually corresponds to increased profit. The local Xynisteri on the other hand, a variety adapted to the dry climatic conditions and less fertile soils of Cyprus, had the lowest PCF (Fig. 2a). The PCF for the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon was in- termediate, as the variety requires more inputs than Xynisteri to achieve the desired yield quantity and quality characteristics. The average yield for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina based on questionnaire answers was 4,240 (+ 1,230), 6,800 (+ 890) and 11,500 (+2,230) kg/ha, respectively.

The contribution of fertilizer production to the PCF of grapes was 0.113 (+ 0.194), 0.218 (+ 0.292) and 0.233 ( 0.081) kg CO2- equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 2b). Significant differences were observed among Xynisteri and the other two varieties (F = 5.78, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2b). Increased use of fertilizers in Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina corresponds to the more intensive management of the two varieties than Xynisteri. Field N20 emissions from the use of nitrogen fertilizers were estimated at 0.061 (+ 0.114), 0.159 ( 0.200) and 0.147 0.064) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions were signifi- cantly higher for Soultanina and Cabernet Sauvignon than for Xynisteri (F = 4.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2c). The increased values for Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina stem from the use of ammonium nitrate and urea fertilizers at higher annual doses than

Fig. 2. Box-and-Whisker plots for grape PCF. Different letters above the box-plots indicate statistically significant differences among the means (P < 0.05) after Tukey HSD following one-way ANOVA. a) PCF, b) fertilizer production, c) field N20 emissions, d) pesticides, e) crop residue management, f) field energy and g) transportation. Note the different scales for the y-axis.

for Xynisteri (Table 1). Emissions from fertilizer production and associated N20 release from the soil have the highest share for the PCF of grape production in Cyprus at 59, 66 and 45% for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 3).

Pesticide use contributed 0.015 (+ 0.018), 0.028 (+ 0.039) and 0.034 (+ 0.015) kg CO2-equiv./kg to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from pesticide use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 4.37, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2d). Increased use of pesticides for Soultanina reflects the extremely low damage thresholds for table grapes. In Cabernet Sauvignon, higher pesticide use than for Xynisteri, may reflect a higher sus- ceptibility of the former to diseases (Table 1).

Crop residue management added to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina an estimated 0.039 (+ 0.056), 0.050 (+ 0.064) and 0.048 (+ 0.043) kg CO2-equiv./kg, respectively. No significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 0.31, df = 2,87, P > 0.05, Fig. 2e), as farmers follow similar practices (Table 1). The worst case scenario for residue manage- ment is to left it decompose in piles, because of the fast release of methane and N20 gasses, which have a greater global warming potential than CO2. Burning of residues is a common practice in Cyprus.

Field energy use contributed to the product PCF 0.034 (+ 0.031), 0.088 (+ 0.055) and 0.343 (+ 0.168) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from field energy use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 74.84, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The increased use of energy for Soultanina is mainly

attributed to electrical energy used for irrigation. Table grapes are irrigated weekly from June to August to increase yields and improve product quality. Irrigation in Cabernet Sauvignon is less frequent than Soultanina vineyards, whereas the local Xynisteri receives irrigation rarely.

The contribution of transportation to the PCF was 0.031 (+ 0.128), 0.026 (+ 0.019) and 0.034 (+ 0.020) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Sig- nificant differences were observed between Xynisteri and Soulta- nina (F = 3.67, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The mean of transportation for Xynisteri was mainly driven by two vineyards (PCF contribution at 0.204 and 0.679 - not shown on Fig. 2f due to axis scale size limitations) with a large distance from wineries. Increased emissions from transportation for Soultanina stem from more frequent farmer field trips to the vineyards for inspections than for the other two varieties (Table 1) as well as a higher number of trips to deliver the product to the markets. In the case of Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon there is only one or two trips to carry the product to the winery and the farmers visit the vineyards fewer times during the growing season than for Soultanina (Table 1).

Only a few studies attempted to estimate the PCF of viticulture (e.g. Kavargiris et al., 2009; Bosco et al., 2011; Point et al., 2012; Litskas et al., 2013; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013; Villanueva-Rey et al., 2014; Pattara et al., 2017), but none focused specifically on the potential of applying management techniques to reduce the PCF. Kavargiris et al. (2009) and Litskas et al. (2013) focused more on energy balance for grape production with emissions from burning fossil fuels only considered for PCF quantification. Although very important, carbon stock balance, N transformations

Fig. 3. Contribution of management practices to the PCF of grape production for (a) the local variety Xynisteri, (b) the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon and (c) the introduced table grape variety Soultanina.

in the soil and residue management were not taken into account. Litskas et al. (2013), estimated the average PCF for Xynisteri grapes at 0.155 kg CO2-equiv./kg, which is lower than the one re- ported in the current study. In another study, Vasquez-Rowe et al. (2013) reported a PCF range for grapes from 0.113 to 1.613 kg CO2-equiv./kg for vineyards in Luxembourg, Italy and Spain, respectively. Diesel fuel (field energy use) and fertilizer use were the carbon hotspots. The increased PCF for some varieties, such as Ribeiro, Cannonau and Rias Baixas was attributed to increased fertilizer consumption. Bosco et al. (2011) reported a PCF range for grape production equal to 0.150-0.300 kg CO2-equiv./kg from the Tuscan area in Italy which was equal to 22% of the PCF of a bottle of wine. In the Bosco et al. 30: The average PCF (+ 1 standard deviation) for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina was 0.283 +0.350), 0.572 (+ 0.580) and 0.846 ( 0.229) kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, respectively. Significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 14.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2a-g). Soultanina had the highest PCF because of the increased use of fertilizers, pesticides and field en- ergy (e.g. electricity for irrigation and fuel for transportation) (Fig. 2b, d, f and Fig. 3). Producers of table grapes use high amounts of fertilizers and irrigation water to achieve the highest possible yield which usually corresponds to increased profit. The local Xynisteri on the other hand, a variety adapted to the dry climatic conditions and less fertile soils of Cyprus, had the lowest PCF (Fig. 2a). The PCF for the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon was in- termediate, as the variety requires more inputs than Xynisteri to achieve the desired yield quantity and quality characteristics. The average yield for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina based on questionnaire answers was 4,240 (+ 1,230), 6,800 (+ 890) and 11,500 (+2,230) kg/ha, respectively.

The contribution of fertilizer production to the PCF of grapes was 0.113 (+ 0.194), 0.218 (+ 0.292) and 0.233 ( 0.081) kg CO2- equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 2b). Significant differences were observed among Xynisteri and the other two varieties (F = 5.78, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2b). Increased use of fertilizers in Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina corresponds to the more intensive management of the two varieties than Xynisteri. Field N20 emissions from the use of nitrogen fertilizers were estimated at 0.061 (+ 0.114), 0.159 ( 0.200) and 0.147 0.064) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions were signifi- cantly higher for Soultanina and Cabernet Sauvignon than for Xynisteri (F = 4.61, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2c). The increased values for Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina stem from the use of ammonium nitrate and urea fertilizers at higher annual doses than

Fig. 2. Box-and-Whisker plots for grape PCF. Different letters above the box-plots indicate statistically significant differences among the means (P < 0.05) after Tukey HSD following one-way ANOVA. a) PCF, b) fertilizer production, c) field N20 emissions, d) pesticides, e) crop residue management, f) field energy and g) transportation. Note the different scales for the y-axis.

for Xynisteri (Table 1). Emissions from fertilizer production and associated N20 release from the soil have the highest share for the PCF of grape production in Cyprus at 59, 66 and 45% for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively (Fig. 3).

Pesticide use contributed 0.015 (+ 0.018), 0.028 (+ 0.039) and 0.034 (+ 0.015) kg CO2-equiv./kg to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from pesticide use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 4.37, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2d). Increased use of pesticides for Soultanina reflects the extremely low damage thresholds for table grapes. In Cabernet Sauvignon, higher pesticide use than for Xynisteri, may reflect a higher sus- ceptibility of the former to diseases (Table 1).

Crop residue management added to the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina an estimated 0.039 (+ 0.056), 0.050 (+ 0.064) and 0.048 (+ 0.043) kg CO2-equiv./kg, respectively. No significant differences were observed among the three varieties (F = 0.31, df = 2,87, P > 0.05, Fig. 2e), as farmers follow similar practices (Table 1). The worst case scenario for residue manage- ment is to left it decompose in piles, because of the fast release of methane and N20 gasses, which have a greater global warming potential than CO2. Burning of residues is a common practice in Cyprus.

Field energy use contributed to the product PCF 0.034 (+ 0.031), 0.088 (+ 0.055) and 0.343 (+ 0.168) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Emissions from field energy use were significantly higher for Soultanina, followed by Cabernet Sauvignon and Xynisteri (F = 74.84, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The increased use of energy for Soultanina is mainly

attributed to electrical energy used for irrigation. Table grapes are irrigated weekly from June to August to increase yields and improve product quality. Irrigation in Cabernet Sauvignon is less frequent than Soultanina vineyards, whereas the local Xynisteri receives irrigation rarely.

The contribution of transportation to the PCF was 0.031 (+ 0.128), 0.026 (+ 0.019) and 0.034 (+ 0.020) kg CO2-equiv./kg for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Sig- nificant differences were observed between Xynisteri and Soulta- nina (F = 3.67, df = 2,87, P < 0.05, Fig. 2f). The mean of transportation for Xynisteri was mainly driven by two vineyards (PCF contribution at 0.204 and 0.679 - not shown on Fig. 2f due to axis scale size limitations) with a large distance from wineries. Increased emissions from transportation for Soultanina stem from more frequent farmer field trips to the vineyards for inspections than for the other two varieties (Table 1) as well as a higher number of trips to deliver the product to the markets. In the case of Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon there is only one or two trips to carry the product to the winery and the farmers visit the vineyards fewer times during the growing season than for Soultanina (Table 1).

Only a few studies attempted to estimate the PCF of viticulture (e.g. Kavargiris et al., 2009; Bosco et al., 2011; Point et al., 2012; Litskas et al., 2013; Vasquez-Rowe et al., 2013; Villanueva-Rey et al., 2014; Pattara et al., 2017), but none focused specifically on the potential of applying management techniques to reduce the PCF. Kavargiris et al. (2009) and Litskas et al. (2013) focused more on energy balance for grape production with emissions from burning fossil fuels only considered for PCF quantification. Although very important, carbon stock balance, N transformations

Fig. 3. Contribution of management practices to the PCF of grape production for (a) the local variety Xynisteri, (b) the introduced Cabernet Sauvignon and (c) the introduced table grape variety Soultanina.

in the soil and residue management were not taken into account. Litskas et al. (2013), estimated the average PCF for Xynisteri grapes at 0.155 kg CO2-equiv./kg, which is lower than the one re- ported in the current study. In another study, Vasquez-Rowe et al. (2013) reported a PCF range for grapes from 0.113 to 1.613 kg CO2-equiv./kg for vineyards in Luxembourg, Italy and Spain, respectively. Diesel fuel (field energy use) and fertilizer use were the carbon hotspots. The increased PCF for some varieties, such as Ribeiro, Cannonau and Rias Baixas was attributed to increased fertilizer consumption. Bosco et al. (2011) reported a PCF range for grape production equal to 0.150-0.300 kg CO2-equiv./kg from the Tuscan area in Italy which was equal to 22% of the PCF of a bottle of wine. In the Bosco et al. (2011) study, the planting of vineyards and trellis infrastructure contributed significantly to the GHG emissions from viticulture. Additionally, the use of nitrogen fertilizers and

N20 emissions as well as grapes harvesting and transport were the parameters that mostly affected the carbon footprint in the agri- cultural phase. Point et al. (2012) in their research for the PCF of grapes cultivated in Nova Scotia, Canada, also reported that fertil- izers and fuels were the main contributors to emissions in Char- donnay and Pinot Noir varieties yielding a PCF of 0.8 kg CO2-equiv./ kg grapes.

Environmentally friendly systems could pave the way for lowering the PCF of grape production. In Spain, Villanueva-Rey et al. (2014) found that biodynamic production resulted in lower PCF and environmental burden than conventional agricultural practices in vineyards. The biodynamic site in the study had an 80% decrease in diesel inputs, due to a lower application of plant protection prod- ucts and fertilizers, and the introduction of manual work rather than mechanised activities. However, carbon stock changes, N transformations in soil and residue management were not taken into account.

Coupling the PCF of grapes with average yield per ha enables the estimation of total GHG emissions from vine cultivation in Cyprus at 2,638, 2,848 and 7,884 kg CO2-equiv./ha for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. Whittaker et al. (2013) used the Cool Farm Tool to calculate emissions from arable crops, either for food or bioenergy production in the UK. Even though Mediterranean viticulture is generally considered a low GHG emissions activity, the PCF of Soultanina in the current study is 1.3 times higher than annual crops (e.g. wheat) in the UK and 1.4 times higher than fruits (plum, apricot, peach) cultivated in the Medi- terranean region (Clune et al., 2017).

Adopting a common procedure for accounting and reporting the PCF of agricultural products enables the comparison among the different products and management strategies. This will be the first step for designing effective policies and strategies to reduce the PCF for the agricultural sector. The accounting of carbon stock changes in agriculture is of major importance for GHG emissions mitigation at a global scale, something that was recently highlighted by the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC, 2015).

33: Farmers answered that they were willing to replace up to 50% of the amount of synthetic fertilizers used with locally produced sheep and goat manure (0.7% N, 0.14% P, 0.7% K). Under the new scenario, farmers add an average quantity of 3,200, 7,000, and 5,700 kg of manure per ha for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. In addition, farmers were willing to reduce tillage frequency by one application for all three varieties (Table 1).

Following the methodology presented in Section 2.2 we re- determined the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soulta- nina under the new scenario at 0.096 0.185), 0.186 (+ 0.214) and 0.507 ( 0.227) kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, respectively (Fig. 4). The PCF reduction was 66, 67 and 40% for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. The reduction of the carbon footprint for Xynisteri corresponded to an average of 0.187 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, of which 0.152 because of increased soil carbon storage, 0.040 due to reduced synthetic fertilizer use and 0.016 because of decreased N20 emissions (Fig. 4a). Emissions from field energy use increased by 0.021 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, a result of the trans- portation and incorporation of the manure to the vineyards. For Cabernet Sauvignon, the PCF reduction was 0.386 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, of which 0.213 because of increased carbon storage to the vineyard soil, 0.117 because of reduced synthetic fertilizer use and 0.072 from reduction of soil N20 emissions (Fig. 4b). An increase of 0.016 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes was observed for field energy use for manure transportation and incorporation. The PCF reduction for

Fig. 4. Changes in GHG emissions (kg CO2 equiv./kg of product) after implementation of reduced tillage practices and replacement of 50% of synthetic fertilizers with ma- nures for the three varieties: a) Xynisteri, b) Cabernet Sauvignon, c) Soultanina. Negative values for carbon stock indicate storage in the vineyard soil.

Soultanina was estimated at 0.339 kg CO2-equiv./kg grape, of which 0.178 because of carbon storage in the soil, 0.099 due to reduction of synthetic fertilizers use and 0.065 from reduction of soil N20 emissions (Fig. 4c). Field energy use remained practically the same under the new scenario (increased by 0.003 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes). Differences in emissions from field energy use for manure transportation and incorporation for the three varieties reflect mostly the distance of vineyards from animal farms. Soultanina vineyards are located in lowlands with a higher availability of an- imal farms, whereas Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon vineyards are located at higher altitudes, where manure availability is lower. The reduction of tillage frequency by 1 application in all vineyards had a negligible effect on field energy use, but contributed to increased carbon storage and reduced N20 emissions.

Currently, management practices (e.g. tillage, type of fertilizers) applied in vineyards result to minimal carbon storage in the soil, which is usually composed of clay with very poor organic carbon content (<1%; Zomeni and Bruggeman, 2013). Even where pruning

residues are incorporated into the soil and not burned, high tem- peratures during the growing period and frequent mechanical cultivation result to rapid degradation of organic matter (Fig. 4). The low organic matter content of soils has consequences for the physicochemical soil properties (i.e. water holding capacity, aera- tion, cation exchange capacity) and for plant nutrient and water availability, as organic matter supplies nutrients and retains water. By replacing up to 50% of the applied N fertilizer with sheep and goat manure (0.7% N), the organic matter content and carbon storage in the soil can increase substantially. The simple step of replacing synthetic with organic fertilization decreases the PCF of grapes by more than 65% for Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon and 40% for Soultanina (Fig. 4a-c). In cases where management prac- tices are relatively extensive, especially for Xynisteri and less for Cabernet Sauvignon, replacing part of synthetic with organic fer- tilizers and adopting reduced tillage practices can get the PCF of the product close to zero.

The proposed management practices (e.g. reduced tillage, use of organic fertilizers) are easily applicable to the important grape producing areas of the EU, as >70% of the vineyards are cultivated under Mediterranean climate (OIV, 2015). In all the important viticultural areas local, well adapted, varieties that usually require low inputs (Litskas et al., 2013; Villanueva-Rey et al., 2014) such as Xynisteri, coexist with imported varieties, such as Cabernet Sau- vignon and table grapes which are more resource demanding (Point et al., 2012). Grapes are often produced in less fertile soils that are marginal for other crops and are close to areas where an- imal husbandry is also practiced. Therefore, animal manure could be easily obtained at no or very low cost. The reuse of animal manure in viticulture will also contribute to better environmental performance and reduced PCF for animal husbandry products. The incorporation of animal manure in the vineyards could increase the organic matter content of the soils and contribute to increased water storage and better nutrient management. However, such changes in viticultural practices should be carefully evaluated prior to large scale implementation, as the quality characteristics of grapes and wine can be influenced by the type of fertilization and other management practices (Schmidt et al., 2014). Although very low or zero emission grapes could increase the marketability of the product, the low awareness of consumers on emission issues in Cyprus as well as in other countries is a hurdle that must be overcome.

Following the methodology presented in Section 2.2 we re- determined the PCF for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soulta- nina under the new scenario at 0.096 0.185), 0.186 (+ 0.214) and 0.507 ( 0.227) kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, respectively (Fig. 4). The PCF reduction was 66, 67 and 40% for Xynisteri, Cabernet Sauvignon and Soultanina, respectively. The reduction of the carbon footprint for Xynisteri corresponded to an average of 0.187 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, of which 0.152 because of increased soil carbon storage, 0.040 due to reduced synthetic fertilizer use and 0.016 because of decreased N20 emissions (Fig. 4a). Emissions from field energy use increased by 0.021 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, a result of the trans- portation and incorporation of the manure to the vineyards. For Cabernet Sauvignon, the PCF reduction was 0.386 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes, of which 0.213 because of increased carbon storage to the vineyard soil, 0.117 because of reduced synthetic fertilizer use and 0.072 from reduction of soil N20 emissions (Fig. 4b). An increase of 0.016 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes was observed for field energy use for manure transportation and incorporation. The PCF reduction for

Fig. 4. Changes in GHG emissions (kg CO2 equiv./kg of product) after implementation of reduced tillage practices and replacement of 50% of synthetic fertilizers with ma- nures for the three varieties: a) Xynisteri, b) Cabernet Sauvignon, c) Soultanina. Negative values for carbon stock indicate storage in the vineyard soil.

Soultanina was estimated at 0.339 kg CO2-equiv./kg grape, of which 0.178 because of carbon storage in the soil, 0.099 due to reduction of synthetic fertilizers use and 0.065 from reduction of soil N20 emissions (Fig. 4c). Field energy use remained practically the same under the new scenario (increased by 0.003 kg CO2-equiv./kg grapes). Differences in emissions from field energy use for manure transportation and incorporation for the three varieties reflect mostly the distance of vineyards from animal farms. Soultanina vineyards are located in lowlands with a higher availability of an- imal farms, whereas Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon vineyards are located at higher altitudes, where manure availability is lower. The reduction of tillage frequency by 1 application in all vineyards had a negligible effect on field energy use, but contributed to increased carbon storage and reduced N20 emissions.

Currently, management practices (e.g. tillage, type of fertilizers) applied in vineyards result to minimal carbon storage in the soil, which is usually composed of clay with very poor organic carbon content (<1%; Zomeni and Bruggeman, 2013). Even where pruning

residues are incorporated into the soil and not burned, high tem- peratures during the growing period and frequent mechanical cultivation result to rapid degradation of organic matter (Fig. 4). The low organic matter content of soils has consequences for the physicochemical soil properties (i.e. water holding capacity, aera- tion, cation exchange capacity) and for plant nutrient and water availability, as organic matter supplies nutrients and retains water. By replacing up to 50% of the applied N fertilizer with sheep and goat manure (0.7% N), the organic matter content and carbon storage in the soil can increase substantially. The simple step of replacing synthetic with organic fertilization decreases the PCF of grapes by more than 65% for Xynisteri and Cabernet Sauvignon and 40% for Soultanina (Fig. 4a-c). In cases where management prac- tices are relatively extensive, especially for Xynisteri and less for Cabernet Sauvignon, replacing part of synthetic with organic fer- tilizers and adopting reduced tillage practices can get the PCF of the product close to zero.

The proposed management practices (e.g. reduced tillage, use of organic fertilizers) are easily applicable to the important grape producing areas of the EU, as >70% of the vineyards are cultivated under Mediterranean climate (OIV, 2015). In all the important viticultural areas local, well adapted, varieties that usually require low inputs (Litskas et al., 2013; Villanueva-Rey et al., 2014) such as Xynisteri, coexist with imported varieties, such as Cabernet Sau- vignon and table grapes which are more resource demanding (Point et al., 2012). Grapes are often produced in less fertile soils that are marginal for other crops and are close to areas where an- imal husbandry is also practiced. Therefore, animal manure could be easily obtained at no or very low cost. The reuse of animal manure in viticulture will also contribute to better environmental performance and reduced PCF for animal husbandry products. The incorporation of animal manure in the vineyards could increase the organic matter content of the soils and contribute to increased water storage and better nutrient management. However, such changes in viticultural practices should be carefully evaluated prior to large scale implementation, as the quality characteristics of grapes and wine can be influenced by the type of fertilization and other management practices (Schmidt et al., 2014). Although very low or zero emission grapes could increase the marketability of the product, the low awareness of consumers on emission issues in Cyprus as well as in other countries is a hurdle that must be overcome.

42: Viticulture, like all sectors of the EU and global economy, need to move towards low emission practices to achieve the targets set out in the EU roadmap to 2050 and the more recent Paris agreement. Accounting of emissions at farm scale is the first step. In the current study, the application of LCA and GHG emissions accounting was implemented in 90 vineyards in Cyprus for the determination of grape PCF. We showed that the PCF of the intensively managed table grape variety Soultanina and the introduced wine variety Cabernet Sauvignon were three and two-times higher than that of the local wine variety Xynisteri, respectively. Fertilizers and field energy use were the carbon hot spots for grapes. Our results indi- cate that replacing up to 50% of synthetic fertilizers with manure and reducing tillage frequency has the potential for substantially reducing the PCF by 40% for Xynisteri and close to 70% for Soulta- nina and Cabernet Sauvignon, through reduction of fertilizer attributed emissions and increased carbon storage in the soil.

Study limitations and future research: The main aims of the study were to quantify and compare the PCF for indigenous VS. introduced wine grapes and a table grape variety. In addition, we investigated the potential of organic fertilization to increase soil

carbon sequestration. While the findings satisfy the main aims of the study, the results are directly applicable to the study region/ varieties tested only. Our findings have opened up a new set of research questions that need to be addressed. 44: Viticulture, like all sectors of the EU and global economy, need to move towards low emission practices to achieve the targets set out in the EU roadmap to 2050 and the more recent Paris agreement. Accounting of emissions at farm scale is the first step. In the current study, the application of LCA and GHG emissions accounting was implemented in 90 vineyards in Cyprus for the determination of grape PCF. We showed that the PCF of the intensively managed table grape variety Soultanina and the introduced wine variety Cabernet Sauvignon were three and two-times higher than that of the local wine variety Xynisteri, respectively. Fertilizers and field energy use were the carbon hot spots for grapes. Our results indi- cate that replacing up to 50% of synthetic fertilizers with manure and reducing tillage frequency has the potential for substantially reducing the PCF by 40% for Xynisteri and close to 70% for Soulta- nina and Cabernet Sauvignon, through reduction of fertilizer attributed emissions and increased carbon storage in the soil.

Study limitations and future research: The main aims of the study were to quantify and compare the PCF for indigenous VS. introduced wine grapes and a table grape variety. In addition, we investigated the potential of organic fertilization to increase soil

carbon sequestration. While the findings satisfy the main aims of the study, the results are directly applicable to the study region/ varieties tested only. Our findings have opened up a new set of research questions that need to be addressed. 46: Viticulture, like all sectors of the EU and global economy, need to move towards low emission practices to achieve the targets set out in the EU roadmap to 2050 and the more recent Paris agreement. Accounting of emissions at farm scale is the first step. In the current study, the application of LCA and GHG emissions accounting was implemented in 90 vineyards in Cyprus for the determination of grape PCF. We showed that the PCF of the intensively managed table grape variety Soultanina and the introduced wine variety Cabernet Sauvignon were three and two-times higher than that of the local wine variety Xynisteri, respectively. Fertilizers and field energy use were the carbon hot spots for grapes. Our results indi- cate that replacing up to 50% of synthetic fertilizers with manure and reducing tillage frequency has the potential for substantially reducing the PCF by 40% for Xynisteri and close to 70% for Soulta- nina and Cabernet Sauvignon, through reduction of fertilizer attributed emissions and increased carbon storage in the soil.

Study limitations and future research: The main aims of the study were to quantify and compare the PCF for indigenous VS. introduced wine grapes and a table grape variety. In addition, we investigated the potential of organic fertilization to increase soil

carbon sequestration. While the findings satisfy the main aims of the study, the results are directly applicable to the study region/ varieties tested only. Our findings have opened up a new set of research questions that need to be addressed. 48: Viticulture, like all sectors of the EU and global economy, need to move towards low emission practices to achieve the targets set out in the EU roadmap to 2050 and the more recent Paris agreement. Accounting of emissions at farm scale is the first step. In the current study, the application of LCA and GHG emissions accounting was implemented in 90 vineyards in Cyprus for the determination of grape PCF. We showed that the PCF of the intensively managed table grape variety Soultanina and the introduced wine variety Cabernet Sauvignon were three and two-times higher than that of the local wine variety Xynisteri, respectively. Fertilizers and field energy use were the carbon hot spots for grapes. Our results indi- cate that replacing up to 50% of synthetic fertilizers with manure and reducing tillage frequency has the potential for substantially reducing the PCF by 40% for Xynisteri and close to 70% for Soulta- nina and Cabernet Sauvignon, through reduction of fertilizer attributed emissions and increased carbon storage in the soil.

Study limitations and future research: The main aims of the study were to quantify and compare the PCF for indigenous VS. introduced wine grapes and a table grape variety. In addition, we investigated the potential of organic fertilization to increase soil

carbon sequestration. While the findings satisfy the main aims of the study, the results are directly applicable to the study region/ varieties tested only. Our findings have opened up a new set of research questions that need to be addressed. 51: Viticulture, like all sectors of the EU and global economy, need to move towards low emission practices to achieve the targets set out in the EU roadmap to 2050 and the more recent Paris agreement. Accounting of emissions at farm scale is the first step. In the current study, the application of LCA and GHG emissions accounting was implemented in 90 vineyards in Cyprus for the determination of grape PCF. We showed that the PCF of the intensively managed table grape variety Soultanina and the introduced wine variety Cabernet Sauvignon were three and two-times higher than that of the local wine variety Xynisteri, respectively. Fertilizers and field energy use were the carbon hot spots for grapes. Our results indi- cate that replacing up to 50% of synthetic fertilizers with manure and reducing tillage frequency has the potential for substantially reducing the PCF by 40% for Xynisteri and close to 70% for Soulta- nina and Cabernet Sauvignon, through reduction of fertilizer attributed emissions and increased carbon storage in the soil.

Study limitations and future research: The main aims of the study were to quantify and compare the PCF for indigenous VS. introduced wine grapes and a table grape variety. In addition, we investigated the potential of organic fertilization to increase soil

carbon sequestration. While the findings satisfy the main aims of the study, the results are directly applicable to the study region/ varieties tested only. Our findings have opened up a new set of research questions that need to be addressed.
